```rust
    tol: f64,
    depth_max: usize,
) -> (f64, usize) {
    let fa = f(a);
    let fb = f(b);
    let m = 0.5 * (a + b);
    let fm = f(m);
    let whole = (b - a) * (fa + 4.0 * fm + fb) / 6.0;
    simpson_integrate(&f, a, b, fa, fm, fb, whole, tol, 0, depth_max)
}

// Integrate ∫_0^∞ by truncating at Xmax chosen from η and θ.
// Tail is exponentially small once x - η ≫ 1 (cf. nondegenerate behavior in 6.11.3).
fn integrate_fd_quadrature(p: FDParams, opts: FDEvalOptions) -> (f64, usize) {
    // Choose Xmax conservatively based on η and θ:
    // • ensure x - η ≥ 20 in the tail → denominator ~ e^{x-η} and negligible
    // • keep a minimum Xmax so that the classical tail is covered for η ≤ 0
    let x_tail = (p.eta + 20.0).max(20.0);
    let x_theta = if p.theta > 0.0 {
        // if θ is large, sqrt(1 + 0.5 θ x) grows slowly; still use x_tail
        x_tail
    } else {
        x_tail
    };
    let xmax = x_theta;

    let f = |x: f64| integrand_fk(x, p);
    integrate_adaptive_simpson(f, 0.0, xmax, opts.tol, opts.quad_panel_guard)
}

// ---------------------- Degenerate expansion ----------------------
//
// Sommerfeld-style approach for large η:
//   ∫ f(x)/(1+e^{x-η}) dx ≈ ∫_0^η f(x) dx + (π^2/6) f'(η) + (7π^4/360) f'''(η) + ...
// where f(x) := x^k sqrt(1 + 0.5 θ x).
// (Referencing Eq. 6.10.11 for the differential structure and the large-η behavior summarized in 6.11.2.)
// We compute ∫_0^η f(x) dx by quadrature (smooth, finite interval), and f', f''' by high-order finite differences.

#[inline]
fn f_core(x: f64, k: f64, theta: f64) -> f64 {
    if x == 0.0 {
        return 0.0;
    }
    let root = (1.0 + 0.5 * theta * x).max(0.0).sqrt();
    safe_powf(x, k) * root
}

// 5-point central stencil for first derivative (O(h^4)):
// f'(x) ≈ [ -f(x+2h) + 8 f(x+h) - 8 f(x-h) + f(x-2h) ] / (12 h)
fn deriv1_central_5pt<F: Fn(f64)->f64>(f: &F, x: f64, h: f64) -> f64 {
    let h2 = 2.0 * h;
    (-f(x + h2) + 8.0*f(x + h) - 8.0*f(x - h) + f(x - h2)) / (12.0 * h)
}

// 5-point central stencil for third derivative (O(h^2)):
// f'''(x) ≈ [ -f(x+2h) + 2 f(x+h) - 2 f(x-h) + f(x-2h) ] / (2 h^3)
fn deriv3_central_5pt<F: Fn(f64)->f64>(f: &F, x: f64, h: f64) -> f64 {
    let h2 = 2.0 * h;
    (-f(x + h2) + 2.0*f(x + h) - 2.0*f(x - h) + f(x - h2)) / (2.0 * h.powi(3))
}

fn fd_degenerate_eta_large(p: FDParams, opts: FDEvalOptions) -> (f64, usize) {
    let FDParams { k, eta, theta } = p;
    let tol = opts.tol;

    if eta <= 0.0 {
        // Fallback if called out of range: use quadrature
        return integrate_fd_quadrature(p, opts);
    }

    // 1) Base integral: ∫_0^η f(x) dx on finite interval (smooth)
    let g = |x: f64| f_core(x, k, theta);
    let (base, c1) = integrate_adaptive_simpson(g, 0.0, eta, tol, opts.quad_panel_guard);

    // 2) High-order finite differences for f'(η), f'''(η)
    // Choose step h relative to η and machine precision
    let eps = f64::EPSILON;
    let scale = 1.0_f64.max(eta.abs());
    // Balanced step: ~ scale * eps^(1/3) for first derivative; use a bit larger for third
    let h1 = (scale * eps.powf(1.0/3.0)).max(1e-6);
    let h3 = (scale * eps.powf(1.0/6.0)).max(1e-5);

    let f1 = deriv1_central_5pt(&g, eta, h1);
    let f3 = deriv3_central_5pt(&g, eta, h3);

    let c2 = (PI*PI/6.0) * f1;
    let c4 = (7.0 * PI.powi(4) / 360.0) * f3;

    let approx = base + c2 + c4;
    (approx, c1)
}

// ---------------------- Public API function -----------------------

pub fn fermi_dirac_general(params: FDParams, opts: FDEvalOptions) -> (f64, &'static str, usize) {
    // Method selection
    let method = match opts.method {
        FDEvalMethod::Auto => {
            if params.eta >= opts.deg_threshold {
                FDEvalMethod::Degenerate
            } else {
                FDEvalMethod::Quadrature
            }
        }
        other => other,
    };

    match method {
        FDEvalMethod::Quadrature => {
            let (val, cost) = integrate_fd_quadrature(params, opts);
            (val, "Adaptive Simpson quadrature (finite tail truncation)", cost)
        }
        FDEvalMethod::Degenerate => {
            let (val, cost) = fd_degenerate_eta_large(params, opts);
            (val, "Sommerfeld-style degenerate expansion + finite-interval quad", cost)
        }
        FDEvalMethod::Auto => unreachable!(),
    }
}

// -------------------------------- Demo ---------------------------------------

fn main() {
    // Examples covering classical (θ = 0) and relativistic (θ > 0) cases,
    // with small/medium/large η to exercise all branches.
    let tests = [
        FDParams { k: -0.5, eta: -6.0, theta: 0.0 },  // classical, nondegenerate
        FDParams { k:  0.5, eta: -2.0, theta: 0.0 },  // classical, moderately negative
        FDParams { k:  0.5, eta:  0.0, theta: 0.0 },  // classical, mid
        FDParams { k:  0.5, eta:  4.0, theta: 0.0 },  // classical, moderately degenerate
        FDParams { k:  0.5, eta:  8.0, theta: 0.0 },  // classical, strongly degenerate (Sommerfeld)
        FDParams { k:  0.5, eta:  4.0, theta: 0.5 },  // relativistic correction visible
        FDParams { k:  1.5, eta:  8.0, theta: 1.0 },  // relativistic, degenerate
    ];

    let mut opts = FDEvalOptions::default();
    opts.tol = 1e-10;
    opts.deg_threshold = 6.0;

    println!("6.11 — Generalized Fermi–Dirac Integrals: composite evaluator\n");
    println!("{:>7} | {:>8} | {:>7} | {:>22} | {:>11} | {}", "k", "eta", "theta", "F_k(eta,theta)", "panels", "method");
    println!("{}", "-".repeat(92));

    for p in tests {
        // Auto-dispatch
        let (v_auto, m_auto, cost_auto) = fermi_dirac_general(p, opts);
        println!("{:7.2} | {:8.2} | {:7.2} | {:22.14e} | {:11} | {}",
            p.k, p.eta, p.theta, v_auto, cost_auto, m_auto);
    }

    // You can also force a specific method, e.g., quadrature everywhere:
    opts.method = FDEvalMethod::Quadrature;
    let p = FDParams { k: 0.5, eta: 4.0, theta: 0.5 };
    let (v_q, m_q, _) = fermi_dirac_general(p, opts);
    println!("\nForced method example → {}: F = {:.14e}", m_q, v_q);

    // And force the degenerate expansion:
    opts.method = FDEvalMethod::Degenerate;
    let (v_d, m_d, _) = fermi_dirac_general(p, opts);
    println!("Forced method example → {}: F = {:.14e}", m_d, v_d);
}
```

Program 6.11.1 illustrates how the theoretical structure of the generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals can be translated into a reliable numerical algorithm. The adaptive Simpson quadrature provides robust accuracy across the non-degenerate and moderately degenerate regimes, while the Sommerfeld-type expansion ensures efficiency and analytic fidelity for highly degenerate conditions. The relativistic correction factor is incorporated seamlessly, demonstrating how the same framework can accommodate both classical and relativistic models without modification.

The numerical results confirm the limiting behaviors discussed in Section 6.11.1: exponential suppression for $\eta \to -\infty$, algebraic growth for $\eta \to +\infty$, and smooth transition in the intermediate range. This hybrid approach exemplifies how asymptotic analysis and numerical integration can complement one another to achieve uniformly accurate evaluations across diverse physical regimes. The modular design of the implementation allows future extensions to include additional approximations, such as continued-fraction expansions or Chebyshev-based spectral representations, for enhanced performance in high-precision applications.

## 6.11.2. Methods of Numerical Evaluation

The generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals $F_k(\eta,\theta)$ are central in physics and astrophysics but cannot, in general, be expressed in closed form. A wide variety of numerical methods have therefore been developed, each suited to different parameter regimes. The efficiency and stability of these methods depend primarily on the values of the degeneracy parameter η\\eta, the relativistic parameter θ\\theta, and the order $k$.

### (a) Series expansions

For small or moderate values of $|\eta|$, expansions of the denominator in (6.11.1) can be used to generate series representations. For $\theta=0$, these expansions reduce to sums involving polylogarithmic functions. While analytically valuable, such series converge slowly when $|\eta|$ becomes large. They are therefore most useful for near-classical conditions ($\eta \approx 0$), where only a few terms are needed for good accuracy.

### (b) Asymptotic expansions

Two opposite asymptotic regimes are particularly important:

*Degenerate limit ($\eta \to +\infty$):* Here the Fermi–Dirac distribution approaches a step function, and the integral can be approximated using the *Sommerfeld expansion*. For general order $k>-1$:

$$F_k(\eta,0) \sim \frac{\eta^{k+1}}{k+1} + \frac{\pi^2}{6}k \eta^{k-1} + \cdots, \qquad \eta \to +\infty \tag{6.11.5}$$

The leading term captures the algebraic growth, while successive corrections improve accuracy systematically. For relativistic cases ($\theta > 0$), the expansion is modified by the square-root factor, leading to the relativistic forms such as (6.11.2a)–(6.11.2b).

*Non-degenerate limit (*$\eta \to -\infty$*)*: In this case the distribution reduces to the Maxwell–Boltzmann approximation, giving

$$F_k(\eta,0) \sim e^{\eta} \, \Gamma(k+1), \qquad \eta \to -\infty \tag{6.11.6}$$

For relativistic corrections, the exponential prefactor remains, but higher-order terms involve modified Bessel functions. For instance,

$$F_{1/2}(\eta,\theta) \sim \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^{\eta} \, K_1\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\theta}\right), \qquad \eta \to -\infty \tag{6.11.7}$$

where $K_1$ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind.

These asymptotic approximations are widely used in astrophysical models, since they are computationally efficient and highly accurate in the respective limits.

### (c) Numerical quadrature

For intermediate values of $\eta$, neither the Sommerfeld expansion nor the Maxwell–Boltzmann approximation is accurate. In this region, the defining integral (6.11.1) is typically evaluated by numerical quadrature.

Because the integrand is smooth and decays rapidly as $x \to \infty$, Gaussian quadrature is effective. In practice, adaptive Gaussian rules or Gauss–Laguerre quadrature are often employed. Special care is required when $\eta \gg 0$, since the integrand develops sharp features near the Fermi edge ($x \approx \eta$). Adaptive methods resolve this by dynamically refining the integration grid around the transition region.

### (d) Rational and continued-fraction approximations

Another important class of methods are rational approximations and continued-fraction expansions.

- **Rational approximations** are usually obtained by fitting simple ratios of polynomials to high-precision quadrature results. They provide compact, fast-to-evaluate formulas with double-precision accuracy across wide ranges of $\eta$ and $\theta$.
- **Continued fractions** converge rapidly in asymptotic regimes, especially for large $|\eta|$, where direct series expansions would require many terms. These are particularly useful in simulation codes that repeatedly evaluate Fermi–Dirac integrals.

### (e) Hybrid strategies

Since no single method is optimal for all parameter ranges, hybrid numerical strategies are widely adopted. In such approaches, the $(\eta,\theta)$-domain is partitioned:

- Sommerfeld expansions are used in the strongly degenerate regime ($\eta \gg 0$),
- Maxwell–Boltzmann/Bessel-function forms are applied in the non-degenerate regime ($\eta \ll 0$),
- Quadrature or rational approximations are used in the intermediate range.

This piecewise strategy guarantees both uniform accuracy and computational efficiency, while also providing numerical stability across all relevant physical conditions.

### Accuracy considerations

- Asymptotic expansions require only a handful of terms to reach high accuracy in their respective limits.
- Quadrature methods can achieve arbitrary precision but are computationally more expensive.
- Rational and continued-fraction approximations balance speed and accuracy, making them ideal for iterative use in large-scale simulations (e.g. stellar structure or semiconductor models).

By combining these methods, it is possible to achieve 15–16 digits of accuracy (double precision) throughout the entire parameter space of generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals.

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 6.11 on the definition and limiting behavior of generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals, Program 6.11.2 assembles a practical, hybrid evaluator that is faithful to the regimes outlined in Section 6.11.2. Different numerical devices are activated depending on the parameters: a rapidly convergent alternating series for the classical, near-nondegenerate case; asymptotic formulas in the strongly degenerate and non-degenerate limits; and adaptive quadrature elsewhere, including all relativistic settings. The implementation emphasizes stability and work-efficiency: it adds a closed-form shortcut at the symmetry point to avoid slow alternating sums, uses compensated summation when series terms decay slowly, and partitions the $(\eta,\theta)$–domain so that each method operates where it is most reliable. The result is a single routine that delivers accurate values across the full parameter range with predictable cost.

At the core of the program is the function that evaluates the integrand from (6.11.1) and a small **dispatcher** that selects the most suitable algorithm per input. The integrand routine applies the classical or relativistic weight depending on the parameter $\theta$ and uses a numerically stable form of the Fermi factor. The **dispatcher** implements the regime selection described in Section 6.11.2(e): in the classical case it switches to the degenerate expansion from (6.11.5) for large positive $\eta$, to the Maxwell–Boltzmann limit from (6.11.6) for large negative $\eta$, to a series expansion for small non-positive $\eta$, and otherwise falls back to quadrature. For all $\theta>0$ it routes directly to quadrature, since the relativistic weight changes the shape of the integrand and the adaptive rule can resolve the Fermi edge reliably.

The series branch implements the alternating expansion discussed in Section 6.11.2(a) for $\theta=0$ and $\eta\le 0$. To ensure both accuracy and stability, it sums with Kahan compensation and uses the alternating-series remainder as a stopping test. Exactly at $\eta=0$, direct summation would converge too slowly; instead the code invokes a closed-form identity that reduces the integral to a Dirichlet–eta value times a gamma factor, thereby producing machine-precision results immediately. That identity is supported by a compact Euler–Maclaurin evaluator for the Riemann zeta function on $s>1$. Two small helpers complete this path: a rising-factorial routine used in the Euler–Maclaurin tail and a numerically safe power function for edge cases near the lower integration limit.

The asymptotic branches encode the leading Sommerfeld-style correction terms for the degenerate regime (referencing 6.11.5) and the Maxwell–Boltzmann form for the non-degenerate regime (referencing 6.11.6). These are inexpensive to evaluate and, within their domains of validity, deliver errors that decay rapidly with increasing $|\eta|$. The quadrature branch implements an adaptive Simpson rule on a finite interval with a tail truncation selected so that the discarded remainder is negligible; this path is used for all relativistic inputs and for intermediate classical parameters where neither expansion is efficient. Together, these functions realize the hybrid strategy emphasized in Section 6.11.2, with thresholds exposed via an options structure so they can be tuned to tighter tolerances.

The `main` module demonstrates the partitioning across representative points. It prints the selected method, a simple measure of computational work (either the number of series terms or the number of adaptive panels), and the final value. Example lines show the non-degenerate tail handled by the Maxwell–Boltzmann limit, the $\eta=0$ case resolved instantly by the zeta shortcut, intermediate classical inputs handled by quadrature, and strongly degenerate inputs evaluated by the Sommerfeld expansion. A short comparison also contrasts the quadrature value with the degenerate asymptotic at moderate $\eta>0$, illustrating how the asymptotic approximation approaches the quadrature result as the parameter increases.

Add the following dependenciess to cargo.toml:

```rust
[dependencies]
statrs = "0.16"
```

```rust
// Program 6.11.2 — Methods of Numerical Evaluation (Final: fast η=0 via ζ, Kahan elsewhere)
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Hybrid evaluator for F_k(η, θ) (Eq. 6.11.1):
//   • SERIES (θ=0, η≤0): alternating series with Kahan compensation (fast for η<0).
//       Special case η=0 uses a *closed form* via Dirichlet–eta & Riemann–zeta:
//           F_k(0,0) = Γ(k+1) * (1 - 2^{-k}) * ζ(k+1)
//       ζ(s) (s>1) is computed via a short Euler–Maclaurin expansion (fast, ~1e-12+).
//   • ASYMPTOTICS (θ=0): Sommerfeld for η→+∞ (Eq. 6.11.5);
//                        Maxwell–Boltzmann for η→−∞ (Eq. 6.11.6).
//   • QUADRATURE (general): adaptive Simpson on [0, Xmax], used for all θ>0 and intermediate θ=0.
//
// Requires: statrs = "0.16" in Cargo.toml.

use std::f64::consts::PI;
use statrs::function::gamma::gamma;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct FDParams {
    pub k: f64,      // order, k > -1
    pub eta: f64,    // degeneracy parameter
    pub theta: f64,  // relativistic parameter, θ ≥ 0
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub enum Method {
    Auto,
    SeriesTheta0,                  // θ=0, η≤0 alternating series (Kahan), with η≈0 → ζ shortcut
    AsymptoticDegenerateTheta0,    // θ=0, η→+∞ (Sommerfeld)
    AsymptoticNondegenerateTheta0, // θ=0, η→−∞ (Maxwell–Boltzmann)
    Quadrature,                    // adaptive Simpson
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Options {
    pub tol: f64,
    pub eta_small_thresh: f64, // series zone for θ=0, only if η ≤ 0 and |η| ≤ this
    pub eta_pos_asymp: f64,    // Sommerfeld if η ≥ this (θ=0)
    pub eta_neg_asymp: f64,    // MB if η ≤ this (θ=0)
    pub quad_depth_max: usize,
    pub method: Method,
    pub eta_zero_eps: f64,     // threshold for |η|≈0 to trigger ζ shortcut
}
impl Default for Options {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            tol: 1e-10,
            eta_small_thresh: 2.5,
            eta_pos_asymp: 6.0,
            eta_neg_asymp: -6.0,
            quad_depth_max: 30,
            method: Method::Auto,
            eta_zero_eps: 1e-10, // treat |η| ≤ 1e-10 as exactly zero
        }
    }
}

// --------------------------- Utilities ---------------------------

#[inline]
fn safe_powf(base: f64, p: f64) -> f64 {
    if base == 0.0 {
        if p > 0.0 { 0.0 } else { f64::INFINITY }
    } else {
        base.powf(p)
    }
}

#[inline]
fn integrand_fk(x: f64, k: f64, eta: f64, theta: f64) -> f64 {
    // (6.11.1) integrand
    if x == 0.0 && k > -1.0 { return 0.0; }
    let root = (1.0 + 0.5 * theta * x).max(0.0).sqrt();
    let num = safe_powf(x, k) * root;
    // stable logistic denominator
    let t = x - eta;
    let den = if t > 40.0 { (-(t)).exp() + 1.0 } else { 1.0 + t.exp() };
    num / den
}

// ------------------------ Adaptive Simpson ------------------------

fn simpson_recursive<F: Fn(f64)->f64>(
    f: &F, a: f64, b: f64, fa: f64, fm: f64, fb: f64, whole: f64,
    tol: f64, depth: usize, depth_max: usize
) -> (f64, usize) {
    let m  = 0.5*(a+b);
    let lm = 0.5*(a+m);
    let rm = 0.5*(m+b);
    let flm = f(lm);
    let frm = f(rm);
    let left  = (m-a)*(fa + 4.0*flm + fm)/6.0;
    let right = (b-m)*(fm + 4.0*frm + fb)/6.0;
    let approx = left + right;
    let err = (approx - whole).abs();
    if err <= 15.0*tol || depth >= depth_max {
        return (approx + (approx - whole)/15.0, 1);
    }
    let (lval, lc) = simpson_recursive(f, a, m, fa, flm, fm, left, 0.5*tol, depth+1, depth_max);
    let (rval, rc) = simpson_recursive(f, m, b, fm, frm, fb, right, 0.5*tol, depth+1, depth_max);
    (lval + rval, lc + rc + 1)
}

fn integrate_simpson<F: Fn(f64)->f64>(f: F, a: f64, b: f64, tol: f64, depth_max: usize) -> (f64, usize) {
    let fa = f(a);
    let fb = f(b);
    let m  = 0.5*(a+b);
    let fm = f(m);
    let whole = (b-a)*(fa + 4.0*fm + fb)/6.0;
    simpson_recursive(&f, a, b, fa, fm, fb, whole, tol, 0, depth_max)
}

fn quadrature_fk(k: f64, eta: f64, theta: f64, tol: f64, depth_max: usize) -> (f64, usize) {
    // Truncate tail at x-η ≳ 20 → negligible (cf. non-degenerate asymptotics).
    let xmax = (eta + 20.0).max(20.0);
    let f = |x: f64| integrand_fk(x, k, eta, theta);
    integrate_simpson(f, 0.0, xmax, tol, depth_max)
}

// --------------------- Riemann zeta via Euler–Maclaurin ---------------------
// Valid for s > 1. Good for moderate precision (≈ 1e-12+).
// ζ(s) = Σ_{n=1}^{N-1} n^{-s} + N^{1-s}/(s-1) + 1/2 N^{-s}
//        + Σ_{k=1}^{M} B_{2k}/(2k)! * s^{(2k-1)} * N^{-(s+2k-1)} + R
// where s^{(m)} is rising factorial: s(s+1)...(s+m-1).
// Use B2..B10; N chosen modest (e.g. 40..80) for s ~ 1.5–4.

// Replace the signature (remove `mut`)
fn rising_factorial(s: f64, m: usize) -> f64 {
    let mut acc = 1.0;
    for j in 0..m {
        acc *= s + j as f64;
    }
    acc
}

fn zeta_em(s: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    assert!(s > 1.0, "zeta_em requires s > 1");
    // Choose N based on s: larger near s≈1
    let n_base = if s < 1.5 { 120 } else if s < 2.0 { 80 } else { 50 };
    let n = n_base;
    let nf = n as f64;

    // prefix sum
    let mut sum = 0.0;
    for k in 1..n {
        sum += (k as f64).powf(-s);
    }

    // tail via Euler–Maclaurin
    let mut tail = nf.powf(1.0 - s) / (s - 1.0) + 0.5 * nf.powf(-s);

    // Bernoulli numbers B2,B4,B6,B8,B10
    const B2: f64 =  1.0 / 6.0;
    const B4: f64 = -1.0 / 30.0;
    const B6: f64 =  1.0 / 42.0;
    const B8: f64 = -1.0 / 30.0;
    const B10: f64 =  5.0 / 66.0;
    let bern = [(B2, 2usize), (B4, 4usize), (B6, 6usize), (B8, 8usize), (B10, 10usize)];

    for &(b, two_k) in &bern {
        let k = two_k / 2;
        let fact = (2..=two_k).fold(1.0, |a, x| a * x as f64); // (2k)!
        let s_rise = rising_factorial(s, 2*k - 1);
        let term = b / fact * s_rise * nf.powf(-(s + (2.0 * (k as f64)) - 1.0));
        tail += term;
        if term.abs() < tol * 0.1 { break; }
    }

    sum + tail
}

// Dirichlet eta via zeta: η(s) = (1 - 2^{1-s}) ζ(s)
#[inline]
fn dirichlet_eta_from_zeta(s: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    let z = zeta_em(s, tol * 0.1);
    let factor = 1.0 - 2.0_f64.powf(1.0 - s);
    factor * z
}

// ---------------- Series (θ=0, η≤0) — Alternating with Kahan (+ ζ at η≈0) ----------------
//
// Classical series (part (a)):
//   F_k(η,0) = Γ(k+1) * Σ_{m≥1} (-1)^{m+1} e^{mη} / m^{k+1}.
// For η ≤ 0 this is alternating with monotonically decreasing |terms|.
// We use Kahan compensation and stop when the *next core term* < tol.
// When |η| ≤ eta_zero_eps, we switch to the closed form via ζ/η to avoid slow convergence.

fn series_theta0_fast(k: f64, eta: f64, tol: f64, eta_zero_eps: f64) -> (f64, usize) {
    if eta.abs() <= eta_zero_eps {
        let s = k + 1.0;
        let eta_s = dirichlet_eta_from_zeta(s, tol);
        let val = gamma(s) * eta_s;
        return (val, 0); // no series terms needed
    }

    // Kahan alternating sum
    let g = gamma(k + 1.0);
    let mut ssum = 0.0f64; // sum
    let mut c = 0.0f64;    // Kahan compensation
    let mut m: usize = 1;
    let mut terms = 0usize;

    loop {
        let mf = m as f64;
        let sign = if (m & 1) == 1 { 1.0 } else { -1.0 }; // (-1)^{m+1}
        let core = (mf * eta).exp() / mf.powf(k + 1.0);   // positive
        let term = g * sign * core;

        // Kahan add
        let y = term - c;
        let t = ssum + y;
        c = (t - ssum) - y;
        ssum = t;

        terms += 1;

        if core < tol || m > 5_000_000 { break; }
        m += 1;
    }
    (ssum, terms)
}

// ------------------------ Asymptotics (θ=0) ------------------------

fn asymptotic_degenerate_theta0(k: f64, eta: f64) -> (f64, usize) {
    // Eq. (6.11.5), 3 terms
    let mut val = safe_powf(eta, k + 1.0) / (k + 1.0);
    let mut nterms = 1usize;
    if k - 1.0 > -10.0 {
        val += (PI*PI/6.0) * k * safe_powf(eta, k - 1.0);
        nterms += 1;
    }
    if k - 3.0 > -10.0 {
        val += (7.0 * PI.powi(4) / 360.0) * k * (k - 1.0) * (k - 2.0) * safe_powf(eta, k - 3.0);
        nterms += 1;
    }
    (val, nterms)
}

fn asymptotic_nondegenerate_theta0(k: f64, eta: f64) -> (f64, usize) {
    // Eq. (6.11.6), leading term
    (gamma(k + 1.0) * eta.exp(), 1)
}

// ------------------------ Hybrid dispatcher ------------------------

pub fn fermi_dirac_fk(params: FDParams, opts: Options) -> (f64, &'static str, usize) {
    let FDParams { k, eta, theta } = params;

    assert!(k > -1.0, "k must be > -1");
    assert!(theta >= 0.0, "theta must be ≥ 0");

    let method = match opts.method {
        Method::Auto => {
            if theta > 0.0 {
                Method::Quadrature
            } else {
                // θ = 0: classical partition
                if eta >= opts.eta_pos_asymp {
                    Method::AsymptoticDegenerateTheta0
                } else if eta <= opts.eta_neg_asymp {
                    Method::AsymptoticNondegenerateTheta0
                } else if eta <= 0.0 && eta.abs() <= opts.eta_small_thresh {
                    Method::SeriesTheta0
                } else {
                    Method::Quadrature
                }
            }
        }
        m => m,
    };

    match method {
        Method::SeriesTheta0 => {
            let (v, n) = series_theta0_fast(k, eta, opts.tol, opts.eta_zero_eps);
            (v, if eta.abs() <= opts.eta_zero_eps {
                    "Series (θ=0, η≈0) via ζ(s) shortcut"
                } else {
                    "Series (θ=0, η≤0) alternating (Kahan)"
                },
             n)
        }
        Method::AsymptoticDegenerateTheta0 => {
            let (v, n) = asymptotic_degenerate_theta0(k, eta);
            (v, "Sommerfeld asymptotic (θ=0, degenerate)", n)
        }
        Method::AsymptoticNondegenerateTheta0 => {
            let (v, n) = asymptotic_nondegenerate_theta0(k, eta);
            (v, "Maxwell–Boltzmann asymptotic (θ=0, non-degenerate)", n)
        }
        Method::Quadrature => {
            let (v, work) = quadrature_fk(k, eta, theta, opts.tol, opts.quad_depth_max);
            (v, "Adaptive Simpson quadrature (finite tail)", work)
        }
        Method::Auto => unreachable!(),
    }
}

// -------------------------------- Demo --------------------------------

fn main() {
    let mut opts = Options::default();
    opts.tol = 1e-10;
    opts.eta_zero_eps = 1e-12; // treat |η| ≤ 1e-12 as zero → ζ shortcut

    // Test points spanning the regimes in §6.11.2.
    let cases = [
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta: -6.0, theta: 0.0 }, // MB asymptotic
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta: -2.0, theta: 0.0 }, // series (η≤0) alternating (Kahan)
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta:  0.0, theta: 0.0 }, // η=0 → ζ shortcut (instant)
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta:  2.0, theta: 0.0 }, // quadrature (series disabled for η>0)
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta:  8.0, theta: 0.0 }, // Sommerfeld
        FDParams { k: 0.5, eta:  4.0, theta: 0.7 }, // relativistic → quadrature
        FDParams { k: 1.5, eta:  8.0, theta: 1.0 }, // relativistic + large η → quadrature
    ];

    println!("6.11.2 — Methods of Numerical Evaluation (Final: fast η=0 via ζ, Kahan elsewhere)\n");
    println!("{:>6} | {:>7} | {:>6} | {:>22} | {:>9} | {}", "k", "eta", "theta", "F_k(eta,theta)", "work", "method");
    println!("{}", "-".repeat(90));

    for p in cases {
        let (v, m, w) = fermi_dirac_fk(p, opts);
        println!("{:6.2} | {:7.2} | {:6.2} | {:22.14e} | {:9} | {}", p.k, p.eta, p.theta, v, w, m);
    }

    // Compare methods at (θ=0, η=2.0): quadrature vs Sommerfeld (illustrate asymptotic trend).
    let p = FDParams { k: 0.5, eta: 2.0, theta: 0.0 };
    for &meth in &[Method::Quadrature, Method::AsymptoticDegenerateTheta0] {
        let mut local = opts; local.method = meth;
        let (v, m, _) = fermi_dirac_fk(p, local);
        println!("Forced {:36}: F = {:.14e}", m, v);
    }
}
```

Program 6.11.2 illustrates the central numerical theme of Section 6.11.2: no single algorithm is uniformly optimal, but a careful hybridization achieves uniform accuracy and speed. Asymptotic expansions deliver high precision in their natural limits with minimal work, the alternating series is reliable and quickly convergent for small non-positive $\eta$, and adaptive quadrature robustly resolves the remaining cases, including all relativistic inputs. The closed-form identity at $\eta=0$ removes a classical bottleneck by eliminating a very slowly convergent series. Because each method comes with a clear error model, the resulting evaluator offers predictable accuracy and stable performance across the full parameter space.

The modular structure makes it easy to extend this framework. One can add Gauss–Laguerre rules for the classical integral, splice in rational fits calibrated from high-precision data to accelerate the intermediate region, or increase the order of the degenerate expansion to tighten errors at moderate $\eta$. In all cases the dispatcher remains the control point, ensuring that whichever refinements are added, the most appropriate tool is applied to each input.

## 6.11.3. Applications in Physics and Astrophysics

Generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals play a fundamental role in many areas of applied physics, particularly in contexts where fermionic particles obey Fermi–Dirac statistics. Their applications span condensed matter physics, astrophysics, plasma physics, and semiconductor device modeling.

### (a) Statistical mechanics of fermions

In statistical mechanics, integrals of the type (6.11.1) appear naturally when computing moments of the Fermi–Dirac distribution. Quantities such as the internal energy, specific heat, entropy, and particle density of a fermionic system can all be expressed in terms of $F_k(\eta,\theta)$ for suitable values of the index $k$. For example:

- The number density of fermions is proportional to $F_{1/2}(\eta,\theta)$.
- The pressure of a Fermi gas is related to $F_{3/2}(\eta,\theta)$.
- The energy density involves $F_{5/2}(\eta,\theta)$.

These relationships highlight why half-integer orders of the integrals are especially common in applications.

### (b) Condensed matter physics

In solid-state physics, Fermi–Dirac integrals describe the distribution of electrons in conduction bands of metals and semiconductors. For example:

- The carrier concentration in semiconductors is expressed in terms of $F_{1/2}(\eta,0)$.
- Electrical conductivity and thermoelectric coefficients involve higher-order integrals such as $F_{3/2}$ and $F_{5/2}$.

Since many device models require repeated evaluations of these quantities, efficient computation of Fermi–Dirac integrals is essential in semiconductor transport simulations.

### (c) Astrophysics

In astrophysics, generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals are central to the description of dense stellar matter, such as in white dwarfs and neutron stars. They are used to compute the thermodynamic properties of relativistic and non-relativistic degenerate electron gases.

- In white dwarf models, the pressure of a relativistic electron gas is obtained from $F_{3/2}(\eta,\theta)$.
- In neutron star crusts, relativistic corrections ($\theta > 0$) become important, and the square-root factor in (6.11.1) ensures accurate modeling of high-energy electrons.
- In stellar opacities and radiation transport, integrals of this type describe the balance between degenerate and thermal contributions.

### (d) Plasma physics

In plasma physics, generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals enter when describing partially degenerate plasmas, such as those found in inertial confinement fusion or stellar interiors. The integrals allow interpolation between the classical Maxwell–Boltzmann regime and the fully degenerate Fermi regime, making them indispensable in modeling electron transport and collective plasma phenomena.

### (e) High-temperature limits

In the non-degenerate limit ($\eta \to -\infty$), the generalized integrals reduce to Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics, which describe high-temperature or low-density plasmas. In this regime, approximations such as (6.11.6) and (6.11.7) simplify the evaluation of thermodynamic quantities, but still provide essential input for numerical models of astrophysical and laboratory plasmas.

In summary, generalized Fermi–Dirac integrals form a mathematical bridge between non-relativistic and relativistic regimes, and between degenerate and non-degenerate systems. Their computation underpins practical models in condensed matter, semiconductor physics, plasma physics, and astrophysics. Accurate and efficient evaluation of these functions is therefore not just a mathematical task, but a key requirement for predictive physical modeling.

# 6.12. Inverse of the Function x log(x)

Many special functions arise as inverses of nonlinear mappings that lack simple closed forms. A classical example is the function:

$$y(x) \;=\; x \log(x), \qquad x > 0  \tag{6.12.1}$$

where $\log(x)$ denotes the natural logarithm. This mapping is continuous for all $x>0$, but its range is restricted to a semi-infinite interval rather than the whole real line.

To analyze its behavior, consider limiting cases. As $x \to 0^+$, the logarithm diverges to $-\infty$, but the factor $x$ tends to zero, producing,

$$\lim_{x \to 0^+} y(x) = 0^-  \tag{6.12.2}$$

At $x=1$, we obtain,

$$y(1) = 1 \cdot 0 = 0  \tag{6.12.3}$$

For $0 < x < 1$, the logarithm is negative, and therefore $y(x)<0$; for $x>1$, the logarithm is positive, giving $y(x)>0$.

The function possesses a unique critical point for $x>0$. Differentiating,

$$y'(x) \;=\; \log(x) + 1 \tag{6.12.4}$$

Setting $y'(x)=0$ gives $\log(x)=-1$, hence:

$$x = e^{-1} \;\approx\; 0.3679  \tag{6.12.5}$$

At this point, the function attains its global minimum

$$y_{\min} \;=\; -\frac{1}{e} \tag{6.12.6}$$

Thus, the range of $y(x)=x\log(x)$ for $x>0$ is precisely,

$$y \in \big[-e^{-1}, \infty \big) \tag{6.12.7}$$

The curve decreases from $y=0$ as $x \to 0^+$, reaches the minimum value $-1/e$ at $x=e^{-1}$, and then increases monotonically to $+\infty$ as $x \to +\infty$.

The qualitative behavior of $y = x \log x$ is illustrated in Figure 6.12.1. The plot makes clear the location of the global minimum, the negativity of the function on $(0,1)$, and the two-to-one nature of the inverse mapping on $(-1/e,0)$.

<div class="row justify-content-center">
    <div class="rounded p-4 position-relative overflow-hidden border-1 text-center" style="width: 70%">
        {{< figure src="/images/pqQDe4beUu67RvW3raYP-fliteLyr26lShoKOYDEc-v1.png" >}}
        <p>**Figure 6.12.1:** Graph of $y = x\log x$**.** The function has a global minimum $y_{\min} = -1/e$ at $x = e^{-1}$; it is negative on $0<x<1$, equals zero at $x=1$, and grows unboundedly for $x\to\infty$. For any $y\in(-1/e,0)$, there are two distinct solutions $x$: one in $(0,e^{-1})$ and one in $(e^{-1},1)$.</p>
    </div>
</div>

As the figure shows, each value of $y$ in the interval $(-1/e,0)$ corresponds to two distinct $x$ values: one lying below $e^{-1}$ and one between $e^{-1}$ and $1$. This observation motivates the need to treat the inverse function $x(y)$ as multi-branched, with the principal branch usually taken for $x \ge e^{-1}$.

It follows that the inverse function x(y)x(y) exists, but with important caveats. Since the mapping is not one-to-one on $(0,1)$, every $y \in (-e^{-1},0)$ corresponds to two distinct $x$-values: one on the interval $0 < x < e^{-1}$ and another on $e^{-1} < x < 1$. These are the two “branches” of the inverse. In practice, most applications focus on the principal branch defined for $y \ge -e^{-1}$ with $x \in [e^{-1},\infty)$, as this branch is monotone and well-behaved.

A closed-form representation of the inverse can be obtained through the Lambert WW function, which is defined as the multivalued inverse of $w e^w$ (Lototsky, 2023). Substituting $u = \log(x)$, we find:

$$y = x \log x \;=\; u e^u  \tag{6.12.8}$$

Hence,

$$u = W(y) \tag{6.12.9}$$

Recovering $x$ gives,

$$x = e^{W(y)}  \tag{6.12.10}$$

Since the identity $e^{W(y)} = \tfrac{y}{W(y)}$ also holds, an equivalent form of the inverse is,,

$$x(y) = \frac{y}{W(y)}  \tag{6.12.11}$$

This provides a neat theoretical solution, but with a complication: the Lambert $W$ function itself is multivalued on the interval $[-1/e,0)$. In fact, it has two real branches, usually denoted $W_0$ (the principal branch) and $W_{-1}$. These correspond exactly to the two inverse branches of $x \log x$ described earlier. For $y \ge 0$, only $W_0(y)$ is real, and thus the inverse reduces to a single value.

Although these closed forms are mathematically elegant, they are not directly useful for computation unless one has reliable numerical routines for $W(y)$. Modern numerical software (Choulli et al., 2023; Gil et al., 2023) makes such evaluation feasible, but in environments where such routines are absent, it is important to develop direct iterative methods.

This motivates the question: given a value $y \in (-1/e,\infty)$, how can we efficiently compute the corresponding solution $x$? A natural approach is to apply Newton’s method, starting from a suitable initial guess (Wang et al., 2022).

## 6.12.1. Numerical Solution via Newton’s Method

A straightforward way to compute the inverse of

$$y = x \log x \tag{6.12.12}$$

for a given $y$ is to apply Newton’s iteration directly to the function,

$$f(x) = x \log x - y  \tag{6.12.13}$$

The derivative is:

$$f'(x) = 1 + \log x  \tag{6.12.14}$$

Newton’s method, defined by $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$, leads to the iteration formula:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{x_n \log x_n - y}{1 + \log x_n}  \tag{6.12.15}$$

This scheme converges quadratically, meaning that the number of correct digits roughly doubles with each step, provided the initial guess $x_0$ is sufficiently close to the true solution.

However, for certain values of $y$, especially near the boundaries of the domain $[-1/e,0]$ convergence can become delicate. When $y \to 0^-$, the corresponding solution tends to $x \to 0^+$, where the function is nearly singular. A poor initial guess in this region, for example $x_0=1$, can cause divergence. Similarly, when $y \to -1/e$, the two branches of the inverse coalesce at $x=e^{-1}$, making the problem ill-conditioned and slowing convergence (Lototsky, 2023).

To improve stability, variable transformations can be employed. Setting $u = \log x$, so that $x = e^u$, transforms (6.12.12) into:

$$u e^u - y = 0  \tag{6.12.16}$$

which is precisely the defining equation of the Lambert $W$ function. Applying Newton’s method in the variable $u$ gives,

$$u_{n+1} = u_n - \frac{u_n e^{u_n} - y}{(1+u_n)e^{u_n}} \tag{6.12.17}$$

Although this formulation looks different, it is algebraically equivalent to the iteration in (6.12.15), and the convergence properties remain essentially unchanged.

A more effective transformation can be obtained by considering negative values of $y$ (where $x<1$). Define $Y = -y > 0$ and set $U = -\log x > 0$. Substituting into (6.12.12) yields,

$$U e^{-U} = Y  \tag{6.12.18}$$

Taking logarithms gives,

$$U + \log U = \log Y \tag{6.12.19}$$

Now define:

$$g(U) = U + \log U - \log Y \tag{6.12.20}$$

Applying Newton’s method to $g(U)$ gives,

$$U_{n+1} = U_n - \frac{U_n + \log U_n - \log Y}{1 + 1/U_n}  \tag{6.12.21}$$

This modified scheme has much better global convergence properties than the direct iteration (6.12.15). The key advantage lies in the fact that the logarithmic singularity near $x=0$ has been tamed by reparametrization. One can, for example, choose an initial guess such as $U_0=10$ (corresponding to $x_0 \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$) and achieve convergence for a broad range of $y$ values with only a few iterations. Each step requires just one logarithm evaluation and a few arithmetic operations, making it computationally efficient (Gil et al., 2023).

## 6.12.2. Behavior Near the Critical Point $y = -1/e$

When $y$ approaches the lower bound $-1/e$, the two solution branches of $x \log x = y$ merge at $x = e^{-1}$. In this regime, Newton’s method converges poorly unless a specially chosen initial guess is used. To construct such a guess, we expand around the critical point by setting,

$$x = e^{-1} + \varepsilon \tag{6.12.22}$$

with $|\varepsilon| \ll 1$. Substituting into (6.12.12) and expanding in a Taylor series gives:

$$y = -e^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{2} e \varepsilon^2 + \tfrac{1}{6} e^2 \varepsilon^3 + \cdots \tag{6.12.23}$$

Inverting the quadratic term for a first approximation yields,

$$x \approx e^{-1} + \sqrt{2e \, \left(y + e^{-1}\right)} \tag{6.12.24}$$

This expression provides an excellent initial guess for the left-hand branch when $y$ is just above $-1/e$. Using (6.12.24) as $x_0$ and applying Newton’s method ensures rapid convergence even in this delicate region (Wang et al., 2022).

By combining the standard Newton iteration (6.12.15), the logarithmic transformation (6.12.21), and the series-based initial guess (6.12.24), one obtains a robust framework that delivers full double-precision accuracy across the entire domain of $y$. Typically, no more than 5–6 iterations are required in practice, making this a practical approach for applications in numerical computing (Choulli et al., 2023; Gil et al., 2023; Lototsky, 2023).

### Rust Implementation

Following the analysis in Section 6.12 of the mapping $y=x\log x$ and its range, Program 6.12.1 presents a practical, branch-aware inverse solver that achieves full double-precision accuracy across the entire admissible domain. The implementation translates the theoretical guidance of §6.12 into robust Newton iterations, selecting parameterizations and initial guesses that honor the conditioning of the problem near the critical point and on the two real inverse branches. In particular, it uses the direct iteration from (6.12.15), the logarithmic reformulation of (6.12.17), and a stabilized negative-(y) transform corresponding to (6.12.18)–(6.12.21), complemented by a critical-point initializer derived from (6.12.24). The result is a compact, reliable routine that converges in a handful of steps for all $y\in[-1/e,\infty)$, including the delicate neighborhood of $y=-1/e$ and the two-to-one region $(-1/e,0)$.

At the core of the program is the dispatcher `inv_xlogx`, which exposes the two real branches identified in Section 6.12 and routes each query to an appropriate parameterization. The branch is expressed by the `Branch` enum: the principal branch corresponds to $x\in[e^{-1},\infty)$ and, for $y\in(-1/e,0)$, to $x\in(e^{-1},1)$; the lower branch corresponds to $x\in(0,e^{-1})$. The function validates the domain $y\in[-1/e,\infty)$ (cf. 6.12.7), selects an initializer consistent with the local conditioning, and then applies one of three Newton solvers. A small `Options` struct controls a relative termination tolerance and a maximum iteration safeguard.

The function `newton_in_x` implements the direct Newton update associated with (6.12.15). It is efficient and accurate whenever the derivative $1+\log x$ is not small, i.e., away from the vicinity of the critical point $x=e^{-1}$. The update uses a step-based relative stopping rule and enforces the positivity constraint on $(x)$, reflecting the domain $(x>0)$.

The solver `newton_in_u` realizes the logarithmic parameterization of (6.12.17) by setting $u=\log x$. This reformulation improves numerical scaling when $x$ is far from unity or when the direct iteration experiences poor conditioning. The function advances $u$, tests convergence on the update size, and maps back to $x=\exp u$ on exit. In the dispatcher, it is preferred near the critical region to avoid an ill-conditioned denominator in the direct update.

For negative $y$ values, `newton_in_u_neg` implements the stabilized transform summarized in (6.12.18)–(6.12.21): with $U=-\log x>0$ and $Y=-y>0$, one solves a smooth scalar equation in $U$ that tames the near-singularity of the original variable when $x\to 0^+$. This iteration is used for both branches on $(-1/e,0)$ and converges rapidly with a branch-aware seed. The seeding is provided by `init_u_for_negative`, which picks $U\in(0,1)$ for the principal branch and $U>1$ for the lower branch, ensuring that the iterate starts on the correct side of $x=e^{-1}$.

Initial guesses are crucial near $y=-1/e$. The function `init_near_critical` encodes the quadratic expansion about the critical point given in (6.12.24), with the sign selecting the branch. This seed places the starting value close to the true solution in the most ill-conditioned region, after which the program prefers the logarithmic Newton step for stability. For large positive $y$, `init_positive` supplies the standard inverse-type heuristic that reduces the iteration count to a small constant. Throughout, the dispatcher chooses between the parameterizations according to the local regime, mirroring the guidance summarized in §6.12.4.

The `main` function illustrates usage across representative values of $y$, including the two-solution region and very large inputs. For each case it prints the selected branch, the computed inverse $x(y)$, the iteration count, and a verification of the equation via $x\log x$. The demonstration confirms the expected branch behavior on $(-1/e,0)$, the coalescence of solutions at $y=-1/e$, and the rapid convergence predicted by the theory.

```rust
// Program 6.12 — Inverse of y = x log x (robust, branch-aware, lint-clean)
// ------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Newton solvers in three parameterizations:
//
//  (A) Direct x-Newton:    x_{n+1} = x_n - (x_n ln x_n - y) / (1 + ln x_n)        (Eq. 6.12.15)
//  (B) u = ln x Newton:    u_{n+1} = u_n - (u_n e^{u_n} - y) / ((1+u_n) e^{u_n})  (Eq. 6.12.17)
//  (C) U = -ln x > 0 (y<0): From U e^{-U} = Y (Y=-y>0) ⇒ ln U − U = ln Y.
//      Newton step (corrected 6.12.21 form):
//          U_{n+1} = U_n − (ln U_n − U_n − ln Y)/(1/U_n − 1).
//
// Near y ≈ −1/e use the initializer (Eq. 6.12.24):
//   x0 = e^{-1} ± sqrt(2 e (y + 1/e))   (± selects branch).
//
// Branch handling:
//  - y ≥ 0: single real solution (principal).
//  - −1/e < y < 0: two real solutions (Lower: x∈(0, e^{-1}), Principal: x∈[e^{-1},1)).

use std::f64::consts::E;

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub enum Branch {
    /// Principal, monotone branch: x ∈ [e^{-1}, ∞). For y∈(−1/e,0): x∈(e^{-1},1).
    Principal,
    /// Lower branch on (−1/e,0): x ∈ (0, e^{-1}).
    Lower,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
pub struct Options {
    pub tol: f64,        // relative termination tolerance
    pub max_iter: usize, // safeguard
}
impl Default for Options {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self { tol: 1e-14, max_iter: 50 }
    }
}

#[inline]
fn is_in_domain(y: f64) -> bool { y >= -1.0 / E }

#[inline]
fn clamp_min(x: f64, minv: f64) -> f64 { if x < minv { minv } else { x } }

// ------------------------- Initializers ----------------------------

fn init_near_critical(y: f64, principal: bool) -> f64 {
    let delta = y + 1.0 / E;
    let base = 1.0 / E; // e^{-1}
    if delta <= 0.0 {
        return base;
    }
    let step = (2.0 * E * delta).sqrt();
    if principal { base + step } else { (base - step).max(f64::MIN_POSITIVE) }
}

// Large-y positive initializer: x ≈ y / (ln y − ln ln y) for y ≥ e
fn init_positive(y: f64) -> f64 {
    if y <= 0.0 { return 1.0; }
    if y < E { return 1.0 + y.max(0.0); }
    let ly = y.ln();
    let l2 = ly.max(1e-16).ln();
    let denom = (ly - l2).max(1e-12);
    (y / denom).max(f64::MIN_POSITIVE)
}

// For y∈(−1/e, 0), U = −ln x > 0 solves ln U − U = ln Y (Y = −y > 0).
// Branch seeds:
//  - principal: U ∈ (0,1)  (x ∈ (e^{-1},1))
//  - lower    : U > 1      (x ∈ (0,e^{-1}))
fn init_u_for_negative(y: f64, principal: bool) -> f64 {
    debug_assert!(y < 0.0);
    let y_pos = -y;
    let t = y_pos.ln().abs();
    if principal { (0.5 * (1.0 + t)).min(1.0).max(1e-6) } else { (1.0 + t).max(1.2) }
}

// ------------------------- Newton solvers --------------------------

fn newton_in_x(mut x: f64, y: f64, opts: Options) -> (f64, usize, bool) {
    let tol = opts.tol;
    for it in 0..opts.max_iter {
        let lx = x.ln();
        let fx = x * lx - y;
        let d  = 1.0 + lx;
        if d.abs() < 1e-12 { return (x, it, false); }
        let step = fx / d;
        let x_next = clamp_min(x - step, f64::MIN_POSITIVE);
        let done = step.abs() <= tol.max(tol * x_next.abs());
        x = x_next;
        if done { return (x, it + 1, true); }
    }
    (x, opts.max_iter, false)
}

fn newton_in_u(mut u: f64, y: f64, opts: Options) -> (f64, usize, bool) {
    let tol = opts.tol;
    for it in 0..opts.max_iter {
        let eu = u.exp();
        let num = u * eu - y;
        let den = (1.0 + u) * eu;
        if den.abs() < 1e-18 { return (u, it, false); }
        let du = num / den;
        let u_next = u - du;
        let done = du.abs() <= tol.max(tol * u_next.abs());
        u = u_next;
        if done { return (u, it + 1, true); }
    }
    (u, opts.max_iter, false)
}

// Corrected (C): U = −ln x > 0, y<0, ln U − U = ln Y
fn newton_in_u_neg(mut u: f64, y: f64, opts: Options) -> (f64, usize, bool) {
    debug_assert!(y < 0.0);
    let tol = opts.tol;
    let y_pos = -y;
    let ln_y = y_pos.ln();
    for it in 0..opts.max_iter {
        if u <= 0.0 { u = 1e-12; }
        let g   = u.ln() - u - ln_y;  // residual
        let gp  = 1.0 / u - 1.0;      // derivative
        if gp.abs() < 1e-18 { return (u, it, false); }
        let du  = g / gp;
        let u_next = (u - du).max(1e-16);
        let done = du.abs() <= tol.max(tol * u_next.abs());
        u = u_next;
        if done { return (u, it + 1, true); }
    }
    (u, opts.max_iter, false)
}

// ----------------------- Public API functions ---------------------

/// Invert y = x log x for a given y and selected branch.
/// Domain: y ∈ [−1/e, ∞). On y = −1/e both branches equal x = e^{−1}.
pub fn inv_xlogx(y: f64, branch: Branch, opts: Options) -> Result<(f64, usize), &'static str> {
    if !is_in_domain(y) {
        return Err("y is outside the domain [−1/e, ∞) of x log x (x>0).");
    }
    if y == 0.0 {
        return Ok(match branch {
            Branch::Principal => (1.0, 0),
            Branch::Lower => (0.0, 0), // limit x → 0^+; change to tiny positive if preferred
        });
    }
    if y == -1.0 / E {
        return Ok((1.0 / E, 0));
    }

    let principal = matches!(branch, Branch::Principal);

    // Near the critical point: quadratic initializer then u-Newton (to avoid d≈0).
    if y > -1.0 / E && (y + 1.0 / E) < 1e-6 {
        let x0 = init_near_critical(y, principal);
        let (u, it_u, ok_u) = newton_in_u(x0.ln(), y, opts);
        if ok_u { return Ok((u.exp(), it_u)); }
        let (x, it_x, ok_x) = newton_in_x(x0, y, opts);
        if ok_x { return Ok((x, it_x)); }
        return Err("Failed to converge near the critical point.");
    }

    if y > 0.0 {
        let x0 = init_positive(y);
        let (x, it_x, ok_x) = newton_in_x(x0, y, opts);
        if ok_x { return Ok((x, it_x)); }
        let (u, it_u, ok_u) = newton_in_u(x0.ln(), y, opts);
        if ok_u { return Ok((u.exp(), it_u)); }
        return Err("Failed to converge for y>0.");
    }

    // y ∈ (−1/e, 0): two branches, solve in U = −ln x > 0
    let u0 = init_u_for_negative(y, principal);
    let (u, it_u, ok_u) = newton_in_u_neg(u0, y, opts);
    if ok_u {
        let x = (-u).exp();
        let e_inv = 1.0 / E;
        let on_branch = if principal { x >= e_inv && x < 1.0 } else { x > 0.0 && x <= e_inv };
        if on_branch { return Ok((x, it_u)); }
        // Repolish if we landed on the other side (rare)
        let x0 = init_near_critical(y, principal);
        let (x2, it_x, ok_x) = newton_in_x(x0, y, opts);
        if ok_x { return Ok((x2, it_u + it_x)); }
        return Err("Converged to wrong branch and repolish failed.");
    }

    // Fallback
    let x0 = init_near_critical(y, principal);
    let (u, it_u, ok_u) = newton_in_u(x0.ln(), y, opts);
    if ok_u { return Ok((u.exp(), it_u)); }
    Err("Failed to converge for y<0.")
}

// ------------------------------ Demo --------------------------------

fn main() {
    let opts = Options::default();

    let ys = [
        -1.0 / E, -0.35, -0.1, -1e-3, 0.0, 1e-3, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 1e6,
    ];

    println!("6.12 — Inverse of y = x log x (branch-aware Newton solvers)\n");
    println!("{:>12} | {:>10} | {:>22} | {:>5} | {}", "y", "branch", "x(y)", "iters", "check: x ln x");
    println!("{}", "-".repeat(85));

    for &y in &ys {
        let branches: Vec<Branch> = if y < 0.0 && y > -1.0 / E {
            vec![Branch::Lower, Branch::Principal]
        } else {
            vec![Branch::Principal]
        };
        for &b in &branches {
            let label = match b { Branch::Principal => "principal", Branch::Lower => "lower" };
            match inv_xlogx(y, b, opts) {
                Ok((x, it)) => {
                    let check = x * x.ln();
                    println!("{:12.6} | {:>10} | {:22.14e} | {:>5} | {:22.14e}", y, label, x, it, check);
                }
                Err(e) => {
                    println!("{:12.6} | {:>10} | {:>22} | {:>5} | {}", y, label, "—", "—", e);
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Spot-check near the critical value:
    let y_eps = -1.0 / E + 1e-10;
    let (x_p, it_p) = inv_xlogx(y_eps, Branch::Principal, opts).unwrap();
    let (x_m, it_m) = inv_xlogx(y_eps, Branch::Lower, opts).unwrap();
    println!("\nNear y = -1/e + 1e-10:");
    println!("  principal: x = {:.16e} (iters {})", x_p, it_p);
    println!("  lower    : x = {:.16e} (iters {})", x_m, it_m);
}
```

Program 6.12 delivers a practical, branch-aware inversion of $y=x\log x$ by combining the Newton schemes of (6.12.15) and (6.12.17) with the stabilized negative-$y$ transform of (6.12.18)–(6.12.21) and the critical-point initializer (6.12.24). This modular strategy follows the computational guidance of §6.12: use the direct update when conditioning is favorable, switch variables when it is not, and seed carefully near the critical point. The examples demonstrate that a handful of iterations suffices across all regimes, including the two-valued interval and very large arguments.

The separation into branch selection, initialization, and parameterized Newton updates makes the solver easy to adapt. For environments with a reliable Lambert (W) routine, one can add an optional validation path based on (6.12.11). Conversely, the present purely iterative implementation is self-contained and suitable for embedded or performance-sensitive settings. Overall, the program turns the qualitative picture of §6.12 into a robust numerical tool that is both accurate and fast in practice.

## 6.12.3. Applications in Information Theory, Physics, and Optimization

The function $x \log x$ and its inverse arise naturally in a variety of scientific and engineering contexts. A classical example is **information theory**, where expressions of the form $p \log p$ appear in the definition of entropy. Determining probabilities consistent with a given entropy often requires solving equations of the form:

$$p \log p = c \tag{6.12.25}$$

which reduces to inverting the function $x \log x$. In such cases, both the Lambert $W$-based representation (6.12.11) and Newton’s iterative schemes (6.12.15, 6.12.21) provide practical computational tools (Lototsky, 2023).

In communications engineering and machine learning, the Lambert $W$ function, and by extension, the inverse of $x \log x$ is frequently employed in solving nonlinear equations that arise in resource allocation and algorithmic complexity analysis (Choulli et al., 2023). For instance, in solar-cell modeling or nonlinear circuit design, one often encounters transcendental equations of the form

$$x e^x = y \quad \text{or} \quad x \log x = y  \tag{6.12.26}$$

where the inverse provides direct parameter extraction.

In optimization and applied mathematics, constraints involving entropy-like terms often take the form:

$$x \log x \leq C  \tag{6.12.27}$$

with $C$ a constant. Converting such inequalities into explicit bounds on $x$ requires inversion, making Newton-based algorithms particularly valuable for numerical solvers.

Finally, in statistical mechanics and physics, the structure $x \log x$ emerges in free energy expressions and partition functions. Here, the ability to compute the inverse with double precision ensures accurate modeling of thermodynamic quantities in both theoretical and applied settings (Gil et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022).

In summary, the inversion of $x \log x$ is more than a mathematical exercise: it underpins models across information theory, physics, optimization, and engineering. Reliable algorithms such as Newton’s method with tailored initial guesses (6.12.24) allow this inversion to be computed efficiently in practice.

## 6.12.4. Summary and Computational Guidance

The inversion of the function,

$$y = x \log x, \qquad x>0 , \tag{6.12.28}$$

is closely tied to the Lambert $W$ function. In principle, the inverse can be expressed exactly as,

$$x(y) = \frac{y}{W(y)}  \tag{6.12.29}$$

with different branches of $W$ corresponding to the two solutions for $y \in (-1/e,0)$. However, since direct evaluation of $W(y)$ is not universally available in numerical libraries, iterative strategies play a central role.

The Newton iteration in the original variable (6.12.15) provides rapid quadratic convergence when a good initial guess is available. To extend robustness, a logarithmic reparametrization leading to iteration in $U=-\log x$ (6.12.21) offers better global convergence. For values of $y$ extremely close to the critical point $-1/e$, the series-based approximation (6.12.24) ensures that the iteration starts near the true solution, avoiding stagnation.

In practice, these combined strategies guarantee double-precision accuracy across the full domain $y \in (-1/e, \infty)$. Typically, only 5–6 Newton steps are required, even in challenging regions. Implementations can therefore be written compactly and efficiently, with modest computational cost, requiring only evaluations of logarithms and basic arithmetic operations (Gil et al., 2023; Choulli et al., 2023; Lototsky, 2023; Wang et al., 2022).

From a computational standpoint, the following guidelines emerge:

1. For moderate $y$: use the direct Newton iteration (6.12.15) with an initial guess near unity.
2. For negative $y$: prefer the transformed iteration (6.12.21), starting from a large $U_0$ (e.g. 10).
3. Near $y=-1/e$: employ the expansion (6.12.24) to initialize Newton’s method.
4. Where available: call built-in Lambert $W$ routines, which unify the two branches automatically.

This systematic approach ensures reliable computation in both theoretical studies and applied models where $x \log x$ appears, including entropy formulations, nonlinear circuit design, and statistical mechanics.

# 6.13. Elliptic Integrals and Jacobian Elliptic Functions

Elliptic integrals form a class of nonelementary integrals that arise when evaluating rational functions involving the square root of a cubic or quartic polynomial. A general form can be written as:

$$I \;=\; \int R\!\bigl(t,\;P(t)\bigr)\,dt \tag{6.13.1}$$

where $R$ is rational and $P(t)$ is a polynomial of degree three or four. Such integrals cannot, in general, be expressed in terms of elementary functions; instead, they define new special functions.

Legendre demonstrated that all elliptic integrals may be reduced to combinations of just three canonical forms, the elliptic integrals of the first, second, and third kinds. These functions originated in the study of arc length: for instance, the circumference of an ellipse involves an elliptic integral of the second kind. They also appear in diverse physical and engineering contexts. The exact period of a nonlinear pendulum is given by a complete elliptic integral, while the magnetic field generated by a circular current loop can be expressed in closed form using elliptic integrals. Applications extend widely, from celestial mechanics to modern engineering simulations, making them fundamental tools in numerical computation.

Elliptic integrals are commonly defined in two forms:

- Incomplete integrals, where the amplitude parameter $\phi$ appears explicitly.
- Complete integrals, obtained when the amplitude spans a full quarter-period.

The incomplete elliptic integral of the first kind is defined by,

$$F(\phi,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\frac{d\theta}{\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \tag{6.13.2}$$

where $0 \leq \phi \leq \tfrac{\pi}{2}$ is the amplitude and $0 \leq k < 1$ is the modulus, a parameter controlling the “eccentricity.” The complete elliptic integral of the first kind is then,

$$K(k) \;=\; F\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2},k\right) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\frac{d\theta}{\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \tag{6.13.3}$$

Analogously, the incomplete elliptic integral of the second kind is,

$$E(\phi,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\; d\theta \tag{6.13.4}$$

with its complete version defined as $E(k) = E(\tfrac{\pi}{2},k)$.

The third kind introduces an additional parameter $n$ (the characteristic):

$$\Pi(\phi,n,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\frac{d\theta}{\bigl(1+n\sin^{2}\theta\bigr)\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \tag{6.13.5}$$

with the complete form $\Pi(n,k) = \Pi(\tfrac{\pi}{2},n,k)$. Legendre showed that these three fundamental integrals form a complete basis: any elliptic integral can be expressed as a linear combination of $F, E$, and $\Pi$ with appropriate parameters.

Although Legendre’s canonical forms are elegant, they can be cumbersome for numerical evaluation across all parameter ranges. To address this, modern approaches often use symmetric elliptic integrals introduced by Carlson. These include,

$$R_F(x,y,z), \quad R_D(x,y,z), \quad R_J(x,y,z,p) \tag{6.13.6}$$

which treat their arguments symmetrically. Such symmetry ensures well-behaved properties under parameter permutation and avoids spurious singularities present in Legendre’s form. For example, the complete elliptic integral of the first kind can be expressed compactly as:

$$K(k) = R_F(0,\;1-k^2,\;1) \tag{6.13.7}$$

Carlson’s symmetric forms underpin the algorithms used in modern scientific libraries (Virtanen et al., 2020). They provide stable and efficient evaluation for all three types of elliptic integrals across the full range of the modulus $0 \leq k \leq 1$, and remain the preferred approach in numerical computing.

### Jacobian Elliptic Functions

By inverting elliptic integrals, one obtains the Jacobian elliptic functions (also known as Jacobi functions). These are natural analogues of trigonometric functions in elliptic systems. If the incomplete first-kind integral is written as:

$$u = F(\phi,k) \tag{6.13.8}$$

then the inverse relation defines the Jacobi amplitude $\phi = \operatorname{am}(u,k)$. The Jacobi sine amplitude is then,

$$\text{sn}(u,k) = \sin(\phi) \tag{6.13.9}$$

where $u$ is the arc length parameter. Equivalently,

$$u = \int_{0}^{\text{sn}(u,k)} \frac{dy}{\sqrt{(1-y^{2})(1-k^{2}y^{2})}} \tag{6.13.10}$$

Two additional Jacobi functions are defined by:

$$
\begin{align}
\text{cn}(u,k) &= \cos(\phi), \\[0.25cm] 
\text{dn}(u,k) &= \sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\phi} = \sqrt{1 - k^{2}\text{sn}^{2}(u,k)} 
\end{align}
\tag{6.13.11}
$$

with identities mirroring the Pythagorean trigonometric relations:

$$
\begin{align}
&\text{sn}^{2}(u,k) + \text{cn}^{2}(u,k) = 1, \\[0.25cm] 
&k^{2}\text{sn}^{2}(u,k) + \text{dn}^{2}(u,k) = 1 
\end{align}
\tag{6.13.12}
$$

Jacobi elliptic functions thus extend sin⁡\\sin and cos⁡\\cos to elliptic contexts, and they naturally appear in solutions of nonlinear differential equations. A classical example is the pendulum: the nonlinear time evolution of the angle involves $\operatorname{sn}$ and $\operatorname{cn}$, producing oscillatory solutions known as cnoidal waves. Beyond mechanics, Jacobi functions are used in signal processing (elliptic filters with equiripple response), in shallow-water and plasma wave modeling, and in many applied fields (Ali et al., 2023; Silambarasan and Nisar, 2023).

### Rust Implementation

Following the discussion in Section 6.13 on the mathematical structure of elliptic integrals and the derivation of the Jacobian elliptic functions, Program 6.13.0 provides a concrete computational realization of these functions based directly on their defining integral forms. While the preceding exposition established the theoretical foundation through Legendre’s canonical integrals and their inverse relations, this program translates those definitions into executable algorithms. It employs the tanh–sinh (double-exponential) quadrature method, which is particularly suited to integrals with endpoint singularities such as those encountered in the elliptic integrals of the first, second, and third kinds. The program numerically evaluates both incomplete and complete elliptic integrals and further demonstrates the inversion process required to compute the Jacobian elliptic functions $\mathrm{sn}(u,k), \mathrm{cn}(u,k)$, and $\mathrm{dn}(u,k)$. By doing so, it bridges the theoretical formulations presented in Equations (6.13.2)–(6.13.12) with their practical implementation in Rust, highlighting the numerical strategies that enable accurate evaluation across the full range of the modulus $(0 \le k < 1)$.

At the heart of the implementation lies the tanh–sinh quadrature method, defined in the function `tanh_sinh_integrate_0_a`. This function numerically integrates a user-supplied function over the interval $[0, a]$ using a double-exponential transformation that maps the infinite domain $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$ to $(-1,1)$. This transformation concentrates sampling points near the endpoints, allowing the quadrature to handle the square-root singularities that appear in the integrands of Equations (6.13.2)–(6.13.5). The implementation incorporates the correct Jacobian scaling and uses symmetric accumulation to minimize roundoff error, with the parameters `h` and `tol` controlling integration granularity and convergence tolerance. This routine serves as a general-purpose engine for all elliptic integral computations in the program.

The functions `elliptic_F`, `elliptic_E`, and `elliptic_Pi` implement the incomplete elliptic integrals of the first, second, and third kinds, respectively, corresponding to Equations (6.13.2)–(6.13.5). Each integral is defined in closure form and evaluated numerically using the shared tanh–sinh integrator. Their complete counterparts, `K_complete` and `E_complete`, are obtained by setting $\phi = \pi/2$ as indicated in Equations (6.13.3) and (6.13.4). This modular structure ensures clear correspondence between the mathematical forms presented earlier and their computational realizations, while maintaining numerical stability for all values of the modulus $k$.

The function `jacobi_am` computes the *Jacobi amplitude* $\phi = \operatorname{am}(u,k)$, which is the inverse of the first-kind elliptic integral defined in Equation (6.13.8). Given a target value $u$, the function iteratively solves $u = F(\phi,k)$ using a safeguarded Newton–bisection hybrid approach. This inversion is robust across the full domain, including regions where the slope of $F(\phi,k)$ becomes steep near $\phi \approx \pi/2$. The helper function `jacobi_sncndn` then derives the three Jacobian elliptic functions, $\mathrm{sn}(u,k)$, $\mathrm{cn}(u,k)$, and $\mathrm{dn}(u,k)$, using their definitions and interrelations established in Equations (6.13.9)–(6.13.12). These functions extend the trigonometric framework into the elliptic domain and are fundamental in describing nonlinear oscillatory behavior and cnoidal wave phenomena.

The `main` function serves as a demonstration of these functions in action. It first evaluates the complete elliptic integrals $K(k)$ and $E(k)$ over several representative moduli to illustrate how the value of $k$ influences ellipticity. It then evaluates the incomplete integrals $F(\phi,k)$, $E(\phi,k)$, and $\Pi(\phi,n,k)$ for fixed parameters $\phi = 1.0$, $k = 0.7$, and $n = 0.3$, confirming their mutual consistency. Finally, it computes the Jacobi functions for $u = 0.8K(k)$ and verifies their characteristic identities, $\mathrm{sn}^2(u,k) + \mathrm{cn}^2(u,k) = 1$ and $k^2\mathrm{sn}^2(u,k) + \mathrm{dn}^2(u,k) = 1$, as presented in Equation (6.13.12). This final step establishes the correctness of the inversion algorithm and the internal coherence of the overall implementation.

```rust
// §6.13 — Elliptic Integrals and Jacobian Elliptic Functions
// -----------------------------------------------------------
// Self-contained reference implementation (no external crates).
// Precision: f64
//
// Features:
// - Double-exponential (tanh–sinh) quadrature on [0, a]
// - Incomplete and complete Legendre elliptic integrals F, E, Π
// - Jacobian amplitude am(u,k) via inverse of F(φ,k)
// - Jacobi sn(u,k), cn(u,k), dn(u,k) from the amplitude
//
// Notes:
// - Correct tanh–sinh needs the step size factor h in the sum.
// - Weight must be w = (a/2) * dx/dt when θ = (a/2)(x+1), x = tanh( (π/2) sinh t ).
// - Stable for all 0 ≤ k < 1. Verified against SciPy/mpmath.

use std::f64::consts::PI;

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
struct Jacobi {
    sn: f64,
    cn: f64,
    dn: f64,
    am: f64,
}

#[inline]
fn clip01(x: f64) -> f64 {
    if x < 0.0 {
        0.0
    } else if x > 1.0 {
        1.0
    } else {
        x
    }
}

// ---------- Tanh–Sinh (Double-Exponential) Quadrature on [0, a] ----------
fn tanh_sinh_integrate_0_a<F>(f: F, a: f64, tol: f64, h: f64, max_panels: usize) -> f64
where
    F: Fn(f64) -> f64,
{
    if a == 0.0 {
        return 0.0;
    }

    let mut sum = 0.0;
    let mut k = 0usize;

    // central term t = 0
    {
        let t: f64 = 0.0;
        let sh = t.sinh();
        let x = ((PI * 0.5) * sh).tanh();
        let theta = 0.5 * a * (x + 1.0);
        let dx_dt = (PI * 0.5) * t.cosh() / (((PI * 0.5) * sh).cosh().powi(2));
        let w = 0.5 * a * dx_dt; // correct mapping weight
        sum += h * f(theta) * w; // include step size h
    }

    // symmetric accumulation
    loop {
        k += 1;
        if k > max_panels {
            break;
        }

        let t = (k as f64) * h;

        // +t
        let sh_p = t.sinh();
        let ch_p = t.cosh();
        let x_p = ((PI * 0.5) * sh_p).tanh();
        let dx_dt_p = (PI * 0.5) * ch_p / (((PI * 0.5) * sh_p).cosh().powi(2));
        let theta_p = 0.5 * a * (x_p + 1.0);
        let w_p = 0.5 * a * dx_dt_p;
        let term_p = h * f(theta_p) * w_p;

        // -t
        let t_m = -t;
        let sh_m = t_m.sinh();
        let ch_m = t_m.cosh();
        let x_m = ((PI * 0.5) * sh_m).tanh();
        let dx_dt_m = (PI * 0.5) * ch_m / (((PI * 0.5) * sh_m).cosh().powi(2));
        let theta_m = 0.5 * a * (x_m + 1.0);
        let w_m = 0.5 * a * dx_dt_m;
        let term_m = h * f(theta_m) * w_m;

        sum += term_p + term_m;

        if term_p.abs() + term_m.abs() < tol * sum.abs().max(1.0) {
            break;
        }
    }

    sum
}

// ---------- Legendre Elliptic Integrals ----------

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
fn elliptic_F(phi: f64, k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    let m = k * k;
    let a = phi.abs();
    let sgn = if phi >= 0.0 { 1.0 } else { -1.0 };

    let f = |theta: f64| -> f64 {
        let s = theta.sin();
        1.0 / (1.0 - m * s * s).sqrt()
    };

    sgn * tanh_sinh_integrate_0_a(f, a, tol, 0.6, 1_000)
}

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
fn elliptic_E(phi: f64, k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    let m = k * k;
    let a = phi.abs();
    let sgn = if phi >= 0.0 { 1.0 } else { -1.0 };

    let f = |theta: f64| -> f64 {
        let s = theta.sin();
        (1.0 - m * s * s).sqrt()
    };

    sgn * tanh_sinh_integrate_0_a(f, a, tol, 0.6, 1_000)
}

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
fn elliptic_Pi(phi: f64, n: f64, k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    let m = k * k;
    let a = phi.abs();
    let sgn = if phi >= 0.0 { 1.0 } else { -1.0 };

    let f = |theta: f64| -> f64 {
        let s = theta.sin();
        1.0 / ((1.0 + n * s * s) * (1.0 - m * s * s).sqrt())
    };

    sgn * tanh_sinh_integrate_0_a(f, a, tol, 0.6, 1_000)
}

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
fn K_complete(k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    elliptic_F(PI * 0.5, k, tol)
}

#[allow(non_snake_case)]
fn E_complete(k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    elliptic_E(PI * 0.5, k, tol)
}

// ---------- Jacobi Amplitude and sn/cn/dn ----------

fn jacobi_am(u: f64, k: f64, tol: f64) -> f64 {
    let k = clip01(k);
    if k == 0.0 {
        return u;
    }

    let k_complete = K_complete(k, tol);

    // Reduce u into [-2K, 2K]
    let mut u_red = u;
    if k_complete > 0.0 {
        let period = 4.0 * k_complete;
        u_red -= (u_red / period).round() * period;
    }
    if u_red > 2.0 * k_complete {
        u_red -= 2.0 * k_complete;
    } else if u_red < -2.0 * k_complete {
        u_red += 2.0 * k_complete;
    }

    let negate = u_red < 0.0;
    let uabs = u_red.abs();

    // Bracket φ: F(0,k)=0, F(π/2,k)=K(k)
    let mut a = 0.0;
    let mut b = PI * 0.5;
    let _fa = -uabs;
    let fb = K_complete(k, tol) - uabs;

    if fb <= 0.0 {
        return if negate { -PI * 0.5 } else { PI * 0.5 };
    }

    // Initial guess then safeguarded Newton–bisection
    let mut phi = (uabs / (K_complete(k, tol).max(1e-300))) * (PI * 0.5);
    for _ in 0..80 {
        let fphi = elliptic_F(phi, k, tol) - uabs;
        let dphi = 1.0 / (1.0 - (k * phi.sin()).powi(2)).sqrt();
        let phi_new = phi - fphi / dphi;

        let mut f_left = elliptic_F(a, k, tol) - uabs;
        let mut _f_right = elliptic_F(b, k, tol) - uabs; // underscore avoids unused warning

        let mut phi_adj = phi_new;
        if !(phi_adj > a && phi_adj < b) {
            phi_adj = 0.5 * (a + b);
        }

        let f_new = elliptic_F(phi_adj, k, tol) - uabs;

        if f_left * f_new <= 0.0 {
            b = phi_adj;
            _f_right = f_new;
            let _ = _f_right;
        } else {
            a = phi_adj;
            f_left = f_new;
            let _ = f_left;
        }

        if (phi_adj - phi).abs() < tol.max(1e-14) || f_new.abs() < tol.max(1e-14) {
            phi = phi_adj;
            break;
        }
        phi = phi_adj;
    }

    if negate { -phi } else { phi }
}

fn jacobi_sncndn(u: f64, k: f64, tol: f64) -> Jacobi {
    let phi = jacobi_am(u, k, tol);
    let s = phi.sin();
    let c = phi.cos();
    let dn = (1.0 - (k * s).powi(2)).sqrt();
    Jacobi { sn: s, cn: c, dn, am: phi }
}

// ---------- Demonstration ----------

fn main() {
    let tol = 1e-12;
    println!("6.13 — Elliptic Integrals via Tanh–Sinh Quadrature\n");

    println!("-- Complete Integrals K(k), E(k) --");
    for &k in &[0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 0.99] {
        let kk = K_complete(k, tol);
        let ee = E_complete(k, tol);
        println!("k = {:>5.2}  K(k) = {:.15e}   E(k) = {:.15e}", k, kk, ee);
    }

    println!("\n-- Incomplete Integrals F(φ,k), E(φ,k), Π(φ,n,k) --");
    let phi = 1.0;
    let k = 0.7;
    let n = 0.3;
    let ff = elliptic_F(phi, k, tol);
    let ee = elliptic_E(phi, k, tol);
    let pp = elliptic_Pi(phi, n, k, tol);
    println!("φ = {:.6}, k = {:.2}, n = {:.2}", phi, k, n);
    println!("F(φ,k) = {:.15e}", ff);
    println!("E(φ,k) = {:.15e}", ee);
    println!("Π(φ,n,k) = {:.15e}", pp);

    println!("\n-- Jacobi sn, cn, dn from amplitude inversion --");
    let kk = K_complete(k, tol);
    let u = 0.8 * kk;
    let j = jacobi_sncndn(u, k, tol);
    println!("k = {:.2}, u = 0.8 K(k) = {:.6e}", k, u);
    println!("am(u,k) = φ = {:.15e}", j.am);
    println!("sn(u,k) = {:.15e}", j.sn);
    println!("cn(u,k) = {:.15e}", j.cn);
    println!("dn(u,k) = {:.15e}", j.dn);

    let id1 = j.sn * j.sn + j.cn * j.cn;
    let id2 = (k * j.sn) * (k * j.sn) + j.dn * j.dn;
    println!("\nIdentity checks:");
    println!("sn^2 + cn^2 = {:.15e} (should be 1)", id1);
    println!("k^2 sn^2 + dn^2 = {:.15e} (should be 1)", id2);
}
```

Program 6.13.0 demonstrates the translation of the theoretical framework developed in Section 6.13 into a fully functional numerical algorithm. The use of tanh–sinh quadrature effectively resolves the numerical difficulties posed by endpoint singularities in the integral definitions of $F$, $E$, and $\Pi$, yielding stable and accurate results even for moduli approaching unity. The inversion procedure implemented in `jacobi_am` and the subsequent computation of the Jacobian elliptic functions confirm the close connection between the integral and differential representations discussed in Equations (6.13.8)–(6.13.12).

The results printed by the program illustrate the numerical stability and internal consistency of the elliptic framework. The precise satisfaction of the fundamental identities among $\mathrm{sn}$, $\mathrm{cn}$, and $\mathrm{dn}$ confirms the reliability of the computational approach. Furthermore, the modular structure, comprising a general-purpose quadrature, analytic inversion, and function evaluation, makes this implementation extensible to more sophisticated methods, such as Carlson’s symmetric forms $R_F, R_D, R_J$ introduced in Equation (6.13.6), or AGM-based algorithms for high-precision evaluation. Hence, Program 6.13.0 not only verifies the analytical relationships between elliptic integrals and their inverse functions but also serves as a prototype for high-accuracy computation in advanced numerical systems.

## 6.13.1. Classical and Symmetric Forms of Elliptic Integrals

As introduced earlier, Legendre’s elliptic integrals are defined through trigonometric integrands. For completeness, their standard forms are restated below.

The incomplete elliptic integral of the first kind is,

$$F(\phi,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\frac{d\theta}{\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \tag{6.13.13}$$

where $k$ is the modulus ($0 \leq k < 1$) and $\phi$ is the amplitude. Its complete form is:

$$K(k) \;=\; F\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2},k\right) \tag{6.13.14}$$

The incomplete elliptic integral of the second kind is:

$$E(\phi,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\; d\theta \tag{6.13.15}$$

with complete version

$$E(k) \;=\; E\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2},k\right) \tag{6.13.16}$$

The incomplete elliptic integral of the third kind introduces a parameter $n$ (the *characteristic*):

$$\Pi(\phi,n,k) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\phi}\frac{d\theta}{\left(1+n\sin^{2}\theta\right)\sqrt{1 - k^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}} \tag{6.13.17}$$

whose complete form is,

$$\Pi(n,k) \;=\; \Pi\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2},n,k\right) \tag{6.13.18}$$

Legendre showed that these three functions form a basis: any elliptic integral can be expressed as a linear combination of $F(\phi,k)$, $E(\phi,k)$, and $\Pi(\phi,n,k)$ plus elementary terms. This reduction theorem is a cornerstone in the classical theory of elliptic integrals.

### Carlson’s Symmetric Integrals

While Legendre’s canonical form is mathematically elegant, it treats the parameters asymmetrically and can be cumbersome for numerical work. Carlson’s symmetric integrals resolve these issues.

The symmetric elliptic integral of the first kind is,

$$R_F(x,y,z) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{dt}{\sqrt{(t+x)(t+y)(t+z)}} \tag{6.13.19}$$
